topics.name: Islamic and Gregorian Months
notes: The Islamic (Hijri) months are based on the lunar calendar, which follows the cycles of the moonThe English months are based on the solar calendar, which follows the sun. A Hijri year is about 354 days, whereas a Gregorian year is about 365 days, so the Islamic year is approximately 11 days shorter. Because of this difference, Islamic months do not align with fixed English months and move earlier each year.For example, Ramadan can occur in summer one year and winter several years later. Therefore, Islamic and English months are not direct equivalents and cannot be matched one-to-one.

rules.title: Masculine and Feminine Nouns
rules.body:
In Arabic, every noun is either masculine (مُذَكَّر) or feminine (مُؤَنَّث). Words ending in ـة (Taa Marbuta) are usually feminine and words without it are usually masculine

This rule can be followed for 90% nouns in the Arabic Language. However, there are some exceptions that don’t follow this basic rule, so for these terms you must simply memorise whether they are masculine (مُذَكَّر) or feminine (مُؤَنَّث)

However, for body parts, there is another rule to consider. Many feminine words do not have a taa marbūṭah (ة), yet they are still feminine. These words often follow a second pattern: if they come in pairs (i.e. there are two of them in the body), they are most likely feminine.

Some examples can be seen below:

rules.title: Masculine = هٰذَا
rules.body: **What to Memorise:** هٰذَا

rules.title: Feminine = هٰذِهِ
rules.body: **What to Memorise:** هٰذِهِ

rules.title: Sentence structure
rules.body: **What to Memorise:** هٰذَا / هٰذِهِ + noun

rules.title: “This is” in the Arabic Language
rules.body:
The reason this is important to know is because Arabic is a gendered language, which means every noun is either masculine or feminine. Because of this, other words in the sentence have to match the noun’s gender.

This matching system is called agreement (المُطَابَقَة - al-muṭābaqah) and Arabic relies on it heavily, unlike some other languages such as English, Mandarin Chinese, or Indonesian.

One of the simplest and most important places where this appears is when saying “this is…”. In Arabic, the word for “this” changes depending on whether the noun is masculine or feminine.

So instead of having just one word for “this,” Arabic requires you to choose the correct form based on the noun.

rules.title: Compound Numbers (21–99)
rules.body: **Rule:** Unit + وَ + tenالوحدة + وَ + العشرات

rules.title: Hundreds (300–900)
rules.body: **Rule:** Unit + Hundredالوحدة + مِائَة Note: The unit will drop its taa marbuta (ةُ) in this case and take a dhamma

examples.english: Note: It is better to just memorise these

rules.title: Compound Hundreds (101–999)
rules.body: **Rule:** Hundreds + وَ + remaining numberالمئات + وَ + العدد المتبقي

rules.title: Thousands (3000–9000)
rules.body: **Rule:** Unit + Thousandsآلاف + الوحدة

rules.title: Compound Thousands (1000 and above)
rules.body: **Rule:** Thousands + وَ + Hundreds + وَ + (Unit + وَ + tens)الآلاف + وَ + المئات + وَ + (الوحدة + وَ + العشرات)

examples.english: = Together:  + وَ + ثَلَاثُ مِائَةٍ + وَ + خَمْسَةٌ وَأَرْبَعُونَ

rules.title: Exact hour
rules.body: **Pattern to Memorise:** The term “hour/o’clock” + Current hour numberالسَّاعَةُ + [الرَّقْمُ التَّرْتِيبي]

rules.title: Minutes past the current hour
rules.body: **Pattern to Memorise:** The Current Hour + وَ +Number of Minutes Past + The term for the word “Minutes”السَّاعَةُ + الرَّقْم التَّرْتِيبي + وَ + عَدَد + دَقِيقَةNote: The “Minutes Past number (e.g. five)” will drop its taa marbuta (ةُ) in this case and take a dhamma

rules.title: Minutes Past the current hour in Fractions
rules.body: **Pattern to Memorise:** The Current Hour + وَ + the Fractionالسَّاعَةُ + الرَّقْم التَّرْتِيبي + وَ + الرُّبْع/الثُّلْث/نِصف

rules.title: Minutes left until the next hour
rules.body: **Pattern to Memorise:** The Next Hour + إِلَّا + The Number of minutes left + The term for the word “Minutes” السَّاعَةُ + الرَّقْم التَّرْتِيبي (القادم) + إِلَّا + عَدَد + دَقِيقَة أو دَقَائِق

rules.title: Minutes left until the next hour in Fractions
rules.body: **Pattern to Memorise:** The Next Hour + إِلَّا + The minutes in terms of a Fractionالسَّاعَةُ + الرَّقْم التَّرْتِيبي (القادم) + إِلَّا + الرُّبْع/الثُّلْث

rules.title: Māḍī (Past Tense)
rules.body: **Rule / Explanation:** Used for actions that already happened

rules.title: Muḍāriʿ (Present or Future)
rules.body: **Rule / Explanation:** Used for actions happening now or in the futureDifferent prefixes show the subject/gender such as a يـ (ya-) at the start if it’s a male or a تـ (ta-) for a female

rules.title: Maṣdar (Verbal Noun)
rules.body: **Rule / Explanation:** Describes the action in and of itself without ascribing it to a subject or person (e.g. walking)

rules.title: How to order a verb in a sentence
rules.body: **Rule / Explanation:** In Arabic, the Verb usually comes before the subject unlike in English where the person comes first then the Verb

rules.title: How to personally possess
rules.body: **Rule / Explanation:** ـي (-ī) added via the letter يـ (ya-) to a noun means “my”

rules.title: How to ascribe possession to him
rules.body: **Rule / Explanation:** ـكَ (-ka) and ـكِ (-ki) mean your male and female

rules.title: How to ascribe possession to her
rules.body: **Rule / Explanation:** ـهُ (-hu) and ـها (-hā) mean his and her

rules.title: How to ascribe possession to them
rules.body: **Rule / Explanation:** ـنا (-nā) ـكم (-kum) ـهم (-hum) mean our your their

rules.title: How to make some nouns feminine
rules.body: **Rule / Explanation:** ة (tāʾ marbūṭa) often makes a noun feminine
